metric_value: Through six months: $31,000 Aberdeen revenue from IBM versus $491,000 for full year 2000.
IBM has been locked down to Aberdeen for new purchase orders for two quarters.
Sales executive Cindy Murray was on maternity leave during this period.
Major account process is surfacing more opportunities into new areas, including a six-figure proposal to IBM's primary market research department.
Aberdeen has suffered from IBM's apparent historic experience that Aberdeen was a white-paper company at a time when white-papers are an IBM expendable budget item.

Despite lack of success, Aberdeen should continue to invest in marketing to IBM — IBM is a leader or player in virtually all Aberdeen practice areas and is an industry survivor; success could leverage to a dozen or more pods.
Goal: IBM revenue into every Aberdeen practice area (pod).
Mix shift from white-papers to ETI content/primary research and Core retainers and consulting.
Grow Aberdeen-IBM revenues to a $750K run rate by Q4-2002.

IBM's revenue run-rate outcomes for the 2001 major account plan are internal proprietary data not publicly available. IBM's total revenue for 2001 was approximately $85.9 billion, declining from $88.4 billion in 2000 due to the dot-com recession. Specific account-level outcomes cannot be verified.
